title: Imported deforestation
subtitle: Imported deforestation is the amount of deforestation in other countries that is driven by the production of food and forestry products that are imported. This is measured in hectares.
caption:
Imported deforestation

Imported deforestation is the amount of deforestation in other countries that is driven by the production of food and forestry products that are imported. This is measured in hectares.

This choropleth world map displays the volume of imported deforestation for the year 2013, measured in hectares. The legend at the bottom defines the color scale, ranging from a pale yellow representing 0 hectares to a dark reddish-brown representing 500,000 hectares, with intermediate thresholds at 10,000, 25,000, 50,000, 100,000, and 250,000 hectares. China is the only country depicted in the darkest reddish-brown band, indicating a value at or near the 500,000-hectare maximum. India and the United States appear in a dark orange shade that corresponds to the band between 100,000 and 250,000 hectares. Russia and Brazil are shown in a medium orange, placing them in the range between 50,000 and 100,000 hectares. A large number of countries, including Mexico, Argentina, and nations across Western Europe, sit in the lighter orange bands between 10,000 and 50,000 hectares. Most of Africa, along with Australia and Canada, are colored in the lightest pale yellow, indicating values close to zero or within the lowest bracket. Greenland, Western Sahara, and French Guiana display a diagonal hatching pattern, which the legend identifies as "No data." The visual distribution highlights a concentration of high imported deforestation values in specific large economies in Asia and the Americas, while vast regions of the Southern Hemisphere and northern latitudes show minimal activity according to this metric.
